message.content: write me a short descriptions of the steps. What we are doing. In simple terms. Also, write me all of the models and technniques we are using

content.text:
## What we are doing (in plain terms)

**The task.** Someone hands you a brain MRI of a patient taken with one contrast (ceT1, the "bright tumor" kind). You have a library of T2 scans (the "bright water" kind) from many patients. Find the T2 that belongs to the **same person**. Do it three times, on three libraries that get progressively harder: registered → deformed → post-surgery from another hospital.

### The pipeline, step by step

1. **Preprocess.** Put every scan on the same physical grid (1mm voxels), z-score the intensities. We don't strip the skull or warp anything — the shape *is* the identity, we keep it.
2. **Four independent fingerprints, run in parallel.** Each branch describes the same scan in a different language, so they fail in different places and we can fuse them:
   - **B1 — MIND-SSC.** A purely mathematical "neighborhood pattern" descriptor. It encodes how each voxel relates to its neighbors, not its brightness — so ceT1 and T2 of the same brain look almost identical to it. No training. Works day one on all three datasets.
   - **B2 — Frozen foundation model.** Run every scan through a big pre-trained brain-MRI encoder (BrainIAC, 3D-Neuro-SimCLR, or M3Ret). It learned general "brain-ness" from tens of thousands of scans, so it shrugs off scanner/hospital changes. Cheap to use, no training.
   - **B3 — Learned shared encoder.** Train *one* network (SwinUNETR backbone, CrossKEY-style training) on the 350 labeled pairs. During training, we *fake* contrast changes and deformations on the same brain so the network learns "ceT1 me and T2 me are the same person." This is the heavy lift; it should be the strongest signal on the easier datasets.
   - **B4 — Shape fingerprint.** Segment the brain into anatomical structures (ventricles, etc.) with a contrast-blind segmenter (SynthSeg), then describe each subject by the *shape* of those structures (SSM). Ignores intensity completely. Great for the deformed/post-op cases.
3. **Fuse the four rankings (Reciprocal Rank Fusion).** Each branch ranks the gallery. RRF blends the rankings without caring about score scales. A target that lots of branches agree on rises to the top. Parameter-free, no risk.
4. **Re-rank the top 10 (Stage 2).** For each query's top 10 candidates, *register* the query onto the candidate with SynthMorph (a contrast-blind aligner) and see how well they actually match (registration residual / MIND distance / segmentation overlap). This is the precision step — it converts "in the top 5" into "rank 1." No labels needed.
5. **Write submission.csv.** 377 rows: for each query, the full ranked gallery.
6. **Local MRR harness gates everything.** We measure ourselves against a held-out 50 pairs from the 350 labeled ones (and a synthetically-deformed copy of those 50 for the L2 case) before burning any of our 100 Kaggle submissions/day.

---

## Models & techniques we are using

| # | Name | Type | Role | Trained here? |
|---|------|------|------|---------------|
| 1 | **MIND-SSC** (Heinrich 2012) | Hand-crafted self-similarity descriptor | B1 — contrast-invariant fingerprint | No |
| 2 | **BrainIAC** (Nat Neurosci 2026) | 3D brain-MRI foundation model, SSL on ~49k scans | B2 — frozen embedding (primary choice) | No (frozen) |
| 3 | **3D-Neuro-SimCLR** (Kaczmarek 2025) | Public 3D brain SSL weights | B2 — frozen embedding (fallback) | No (frozen) |
| 4 | **M3Ret** (Liu 2025) | Multimodal medical retrieval encoder | B2 — frozen embedding (fallback) | No (frozen) |
| 5 | **CrossKEY** (Morozov & Dorent 2025) | Matching-by-synthesis + supervised contrastive + rotation-invariant keypoint descriptor. **By the challenge PI.** | B3 — training recipe we adapt from MR↔US to ceT1↔T2 | Yes (adapted & trained) |
| 6 | **SwinUNETR** (MONAI) | 3D Swin-Transformer encoder | B3 — backbone, init from MONAI `model_swinvit.pt`, reused from `~/INTERNSHIP` | Yes (fine-tuned) |
| 7 | **SynthMorph / SynthSeg-style augmentation** (Hoffmann/Billot) | Generative contrast synthesis from label maps | B3 augmentation — manufactures contrast invariance during training | Used as augmentor |
| 8 | **Random rigid + nonlinear deformation aug** | Standard 3D MONAI transforms, applied **independently per view** | B3 augmentation — manufactures deformation invariance | Used as augmentor |
| 9 | **Supervised contrastive loss** (with curriculum triplet + hard-negative mining, from CrossKEY) | Loss function | B3 — pulls same-subject ceT1/T2 together, pushes others apart | — |
| 10 | **SynthSeg** | Contrast-agnostic segmenter | B4 — turns intensity volume into anatomical label map | No (frozen) |
| 11 | **SSM (Statistical Shape Model)** | Shape descriptors over segmented structures, reused from `~/INTERNSHIP/modeling/SSM` | B4 — shape fingerprint | Fit on the data |
| 12 | **Reciprocal Rank Fusion** (Cormack 2009) | Rank aggregation, parameter-free | Stage 1 fusion of B1–B4 | — |
| 13 | **SynthMorph** (registration mode) | Contrast-invariant 3D image registration network | Stage 2 — register query onto each top-k candidate | No (frozen) |
| 14 | **MIND distance** | Same MIND descriptor used as a similarity metric post-registration | Stage 2 scoring | — |
| 15 | **Segmentation overlap (Dice)** | Compare SynthSeg label maps of registered query vs candidate | Stage 2 scoring | — |
| 16 | **C-MIR ColBERT-style late interaction** (Khun Jush 2025) | Set-of-local-features re-ranker | Stage 2 *stretch* if SynthMorph re-rank still has headroom | No (stretch) |
| 17 | **MedGemma latent harness** (Alex's prior work) | `latents.csv` extraction contract + UMAP + `--residualize covariates` | Sanity checks for B2/B3 embeddings; remove nuisance (scanner/contrast) so subject identity dominates | — |
| 18 | **MONAI** (pipeline) | 3D medical imaging deep learning library | Data loading, caching (`PersistentDataset`), transforms, SwinUNETR | — |
| 19 | **PyTorch on ROCm** | Framework, MI300X compatible | All deep learning | — |

content.text:
Tasks queued. Here's the plain English on **your part** and where to start.

## Your role on this team

You own **Track A: the two hardest, deepest-modeling branches** — the learned encoder (B3) and the shape fingerprint (B4). You were picked for this because you know MONAI, 3D MRI, SwinUNETR, and SSM cold, and the rest of the team doesn't. The other two are doing classical CV (Track B) and infrastructure + foundation embeddings (Track C).

**But you do NOT start on your own track.** You start by helping the team not implode in the first two hours.

## Hours 0–2 — the shared sprint (everyone helps)

The whole 24h falls apart if Track C's eval harness doesn't land in the first two hours. Without it, nobody can tell if their branch is working. So your H0–2 is:

1. **Boot one MI300X, prove ROCm works.** Just one. Install PyTorch ROCm + MONAI, run a tiny conv on the GPU, done. (Task #1.) Don't bother optimizing — you're just checking the rails aren't broken.
2. **Clone CrossKEY and the challenge repo.** Get the data downloaded, `DATA_ROOT` set, run the baseline once to confirm everything connects. (Task #2.)
3. **Pull the SwinUNETR code + weights from INTERNSHIP.** Confirm they load on ROCm. If they don't, you need to know now, not at hour 5. (Task #3.)
4. **Pair-program the 3D data loader with C.** This is the big one. C is owning the harness but you're the only person on the team who's loaded 3D NIfTIs in MONAI before. Build the loader together — read `.nii.gz`, resample to 1mm, pad/crop, z-score, batch. It'll get reused by **every branch**, so get it right once. (Task #4.) **Don't leave to start your own work until C can compute MRR on a held-out split.**

## Hours 2–10 — your real track starts (B3)

Once the harness is alive:

5. **Build the augmenter.** This is the secret sauce of B3. For each ceT1↔T2 pair, you apply *fake* contrast remapping and *fake* deformations **independently to each view**. The point: teach the network that "ceT1 me" and "T2 me" are the same person, even when warped. (Task #5.) Eyeball 10 augmented pairs before training — if they don't look like wildly different scans of the same brain, the augmenter is broken.
6. **Smoke-train for 30 min.** Tiny SwinUNETR, 1 epoch, just confirm the loss curve goes down. (Task #6.) This is a sanity check, not a result. If it crashes or NaNs, you find out cheap.
7. **Full B3 training.** CrossKEY recipe: matching-by-synthesis + supervised contrastive + curriculum triplet loss with hard-negative mining. Checkpoint every hour so you always have a fallback. (Task #7.)
8. **Ship the B3 ranking CSV to C.** Embed every query and every gallery scan, cosine-rank, dump in the agreed `{query_id, target_id, score}` format. (Task #8.) C plugs it into the RRF combiner.

## Hours 10–18 — B4 (the shape branch)

9. **SynthSeg on every volume**, cache the label maps. Slow but parallelizable across MI300X instances. (Task #9.)
10. **SSM shape features** from those label maps using your INTERNSHIP code. Per-subject vector, cosine-rank, hand to C. (Task #10.)

## The mental model

- **Track A is the heaviest track. That's why it's yours.**
- **B3 is the single biggest possible lever** on dataset1/2. If it works, we win.
- **B3 is also the most likely to not converge in time.** That's fine — the floor is B1+B2+RRF+Stage-2 (the other two tracks). B3 is upside, not a dependency. Don't burn time perfecting B3 if it's flat at hour 8 — pivot to B4.
- **Every branch hands C a ranking CSV.** You never write to `submission.csv` directly. C is the gate.
- **Measure locally before you ship anywhere.** We have 100 Kaggle submissions/day. Don't waste them.

## Start now with Task #1

Boot one MI300X and confirm `import torch; torch.cuda.is_available()` returns True. That's the only decision-blocking thing right now — if ROCm is broken on these instances, the whole plan changes and we want to know in the first hour.

message.content: should we pull the weights from the SWINUNTER internship? would that help? It is on knee mri

content.text:
**Short answer: no.** Don't init B3 from the INTERNSHIP knee weights. Use the INTERNSHIP **code**, but use **MONAI's brain-relevant pretrained weights** for initialization.

## Why knee → brain is a weak transfer

- **Different anatomy.** Cortical bone, meniscus, cartilage vs gray matter, white matter, ventricles. The early-layer filters learned on knee texture won't match brain texture.
- **Different sequences.** OA progression uses PD/T2-FS/T1; you have ceT1 and T2. The contrast statistics the network learned are wrong.
- **Different feature scales.** Knee structures are cm-scale chunks; brain has fine cortical folds.
- **Fine-tuned weights are *more* specialized than SSL weights.** The knee checkpoint was tuned for an OA task — it has actively *unlearned* features that don't help OA prediction. SSL weights from a general 3D-imaging corpus are a better prior.

What you'd actually be transferring is the **SwinUNETR architecture** itself, which is the same whether you init from random, knee, or MONAI's pretrained. The architecture is free; the weights matter.

## What to use instead

The ROADMAP already calls for this — `init from MONAI pretrained model_swinvit.pt`. That's the public SwinUNETR SSL checkpoint pretrained on ~5k 3D CT volumes (head/abdomen). Not brain-perfect, but far closer than knee, and it's the standard reference so your B3 results will be comparable.

If you have time and want to be fancier: there's **3D-Neuro-SimCLR / BrainIAC** weights pretrained specifically on 3D brain MRI. But:
- Those are *different architectures* (likely ResNet3D, not SwinUNETR), so they don't drop into your backbone.
- They're already in the plan as **B2 (frozen foundation embedding)** — a separate parallel branch. Don't double-dip.

## What IS useful from INTERNSHIP

The **wrapper code** — model instantiation, MONAI integration, training-loop scaffolding (`seg_swinunetr.py`, the Swin/ folder). Plus the medgemma `latents.csv` contract and the SSM pipeline. **Code reuses; weights don't.**

Let me sharpen Task #3 to reflect this.

Do NOT use the OA/knee fine-tuned checkpoints (wrong anatomy, wrong sequences, actively unhelpful for brain). Init the backbone from MONAI's public `model_swinvit.pt` (SwinUNETR SSL on ~5k 3D CT volumes) — closer to brain than knee, and it's the standard reference. Confirm the model instantiates on ROCm and the MONAI weights load without shape mismatches.

message.content: okay, let's start with the SMOKE-Test ROCm. What is ROCm?

content.text:
## What ROCm is, in one paragraph

**ROCm = Radeon Open Compute.** It's AMD's version of CUDA — the software stack (driver + runtime + compiler + libraries) that lets you actually run GPU code on AMD cards. The MI300X is an AMD GPU, so it doesn't speak CUDA; it speaks ROCm. PyTorch ships pre-built "ROCm wheels" that map the same PyTorch API onto AMD hardware, so 95% of your code works unchanged. Confusingly, even on ROCm you still call `torch.cuda.is_available()` — the API name was kept for compatibility. The equivalent of `nvidia-smi` is `rocm-smi`.

**The catch:** a few libraries depend on custom CUDA kernels (FlashAttention, some xformers ops, occasionally MONAI extras). On ROCm they either work via a ROCm port (most do now), fall back to a slower path, or fail loudly. That's exactly what this smoke test is checking.

## Start Task #1

I can't reach an MI300X from this session — you have to SSH in and run things. I'll mark the task in-progress and give you the exact commands; paste the outputs back here and we'll go from there.

content.content: Updated task #1 status

content.text:
### On one MI300X instance, run these in order:

**1. Confirm the GPU is visible to the OS:**
```bash
rocm-smi
```
You should see one (or more) MI300X listed with 192 GB memory. If `rocm-smi` is missing → ROCm itself isn't installed; we install it before PyTorch.

**2. Install a clean PyTorch ROCm wheel.** Use a fresh venv so we don't fight system packages:
```bash
python -m venv ~/ehl-venv && source ~/ehl-venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip
# ROCm 6.2 wheel — adjust if the box has a different ROCm version (check with: cat /opt/rocm/.info/version)
pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/rocm6.2
```

**3. The smoke test itself:**
```bash
python - <<'EOF'
import torch
print("torch:", torch.__version__)
print("cuda available:", torch.cuda.is_available())          # True on ROCm
print("device count:", torch.cuda.device_count())
print("device name:", torch.cuda.get_device_name(0) if torch.cuda.is_available() else "—")
x = torch.randn(2048, 2048, device="cuda")
y = x @ x
torch.cuda.synchronize()
print("matmul ok, result mean:", y.mean().item())
EOF
```

**What "passes" looks like:**
- `cuda available: True`
- `device name:` contains `MI300X` (or `gfx942`)
- `matmul ok` prints without error

**4. Install MONAI on top** (we'll need it next):
```bash
pip install monai nibabel numpy tqdm scikit-learn
python -c "import monai; print('monai:', monai.__version__)"
```

Paste the output of steps 1, 3, and 4 back here. If anything errors, we debug before touching the next task.

Good — Jupyter on the MI300X is fine. You don't need to share the URL/token/password with me (I can't reach the box anyway, and you shouldn't paste secrets here).

## What to do

1. **Open the Jupyter URL in your browser, paste the token.**
2. From the Launcher, open **Terminal** (easiest — same bash commands as before) **or** a **Python 3 notebook** (prefix bash with `!`).

### If you opened a Terminal
Run the four steps from the previous message exactly as written.

### If you opened a Notebook
Cell 1 (bash — note the `!`):
```bash
!rocm-smi
!cat /opt/rocm/.info/version 2>/dev/null || echo "no /opt/rocm/.info/version"
```

Cell 2 (bash — venv + install). Run once:
```bash
!python -m venv ~/ehl-venv
!~/ehl-venv/bin/pip install --upgrade pip
!~/ehl-venv/bin/pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/rocm6.2
!~/ehl-venv/bin/pip install monai nibabel numpy tqdm scikit-learn
```

> ⚠️ Notebook kernels run in whatever Python launched Jupyter, **not** the venv. For the actual smoke test, either (a) run it as bash via the venv's python, or (b) install ipykernel into the venv and switch the kernel. (a) is lazier:

Cell 3 (bash — the actual smoke test):
```bash
!~/ehl-venv/bin/python - <<'EOF'
import torch
print("torch:", torch.__version__)
print("cuda available:", torch.cuda.is_available())
print("device count:", torch.cuda.device_count())
print("device name:", torch.cuda.get_device_name(0) if torch.cuda.is_available() else "—")
x = torch.randn(2048, 2048, device="cuda")
y = x @ x
torch.cuda.synchronize()
print("matmul ok, result mean:", y.mean().item())
import monai; print("monai:", monai.__version__)
EOF
```

Paste the output of Cell 1 and Cell 3 back. If `rocm-smi` is missing or the ROCm version isn't 6.2, tell me what it says and I'll adjust the wheel index URL.

content.text:
The `!` is only for Jupyter notebook cells. In a terminal, drop it. Bash interpreted `!rocm` as a history-expansion request, hence "event not found".

Run these instead (no `!`):

```bash
rocm-smi
cat /opt/rocm/.info/version 2>/dev/null || echo "no /opt/rocm/.info/version"
```

Side note: your prompt is `root@13ebd1a804d0:/app#` — that 12-char hostname is the Docker container ID format. You're inside a container, not on the bare-metal host. Good news: ROCm devices and Python are almost certainly pre-installed in the image — check before installing anything:

```bash
which python python3 pip
python3 -c "import torch; print(torch.__version__, torch.cuda.is_available())" 2>&1 | head -20
```

Paste those outputs. If torch + ROCm are already there, we skip the venv install entirely.

content.text:
ROCm and PyTorch are already working in the container — that saves us ~20 minutes of install. Quick read:

- **GPU:** 1× MI300X (`0x74b5`), idle. ROCm **7.2.3** (newer than I guessed).
- **PyTorch:** 2.10.0 nightly, already built against this ROCm, `cuda.is_available() == True`. No install needed.
- **Container has 1 GPU.** The "38 instances" means 38 separate containers, each with one GPU — that's actually ideal for our plan (one branch per instance).

Now the actual matmul + library check. Run this — no `!`, just bash:

```bash
python3 - <<'EOF'
import torch
print("torch:", torch.__version__)
print("device count:", torch.cuda.device_count())
print("device name:", torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))
print("hip:", torch.version.hip)
x = torch.randn(4096, 4096, device="cuda")
y = x @ x
torch.cuda.synchronize()
print("matmul ok, mean:", y.mean().item(), "free GB:", torch.cuda.mem_get_info()[0] / 1e9)
EOF
```

```bash
python3 -c "import monai, nibabel, numpy, sklearn, tqdm; print('monai', monai.__version__, '| nibabel', nibabel.__version__, '| numpy', numpy.__version__, '| sklearn', sklearn.__version__)" 2>&1
```

Paste both. If MONAI / nibabel are missing, we'll install with `pip install monai nibabel` (system pip is fine — no venv needed in a disposable container).

content.text:
### Step A — find the persistent mount

```bash
df -h
mount | grep -vE 'tmpfs|overlay|proc|sysfs|cgroup|devpts|mqueue|shm'
```

Look for something mounted at `/data`, `/workspace`, `/mnt/...`, or similar with lots of free GB. Paste the output and I'll point at the right path.

### Step B — Kaggle credentials

You need a `kaggle.json` API token (get it from kaggle.com → your account → "Create New API Token" — it downloads a `kaggle.json` file). Get that file onto the container *first*, then:

```bash
pip install kaggle
mkdir -p ~/.kaggle
# paste your kaggle.json into ~/.kaggle/kaggle.json (or scp it in)
chmod 600 ~/.kaggle/kaggle.json
kaggle --version
```

### Step C — once df + kaggle are ready, paste outputs

Once we know the persistent path and `kaggle --version` works, I'll give you the one-liner that:
- creates `DATA_ROOT` on the persistent volume,
- downloads the competition zip with `kaggle competitions download -c <slug>`,
- unzips,
- clones both repos next to it,
- runs the baseline once to confirm the data layout matches what the code expects.

Skip nothing — paste `df -h` first so we put the data where it'll survive a container restart.

# Brain MRI Cross-Modal Retrieval Challenge

Your task is to build a cross-modal medical image retrieval system. For each query brain MRI volume, rank all candidate target MRI volumes from the matching gallery so that the true same-subject target appears as high as possible.

## Kaggle Challenge

https://www.kaggle.com/t/b33ec3e76c3d4e16a6b56852470b3ebf


## Modalities

- Query: T1 post-contrast MRI
- Target: T2 MRI

All images are 3D NIfTI `.nii.gz` volumes converted to RAS orientation.

## Data Layout

The data is split into three independent datasets:

```text
dataset1/
  train_pairs.csv
  val_queries.csv
  val_gallery.csv
  test_queries.csv
  test_gallery.csv
  images/
    train/
    val/
    test/

dataset2/
  val_queries.csv
  val_gallery.csv
  test_queries.csv
  test_gallery.csv
  images/
    val/
    test/

dataset3/
  val_queries.csv
  val_gallery.csv
  test_queries.csv
  test_gallery.csv
  images/
    val/
    test/

sample_submission.csv
```

`dataset1` includes labelled training pairs. `dataset2` and `dataset3` have no labelled training pairs and are intended to evaluate generalization.

## Dataset Descriptions

### Dataset 1

`dataset1` contains preoperative MRI pairs only. It provides the labelled training set for the challenge: each row in `dataset1/train_pairs.csv` gives a matching T1 post-contrast query image and T2 target image from the same subject.

All `dataset1` pairs, including training, validation, and test pairs, are registered to a common image grid. You may use that fact when training on the labelled pairs and when developing methods on this dataset.

`dataset1` validation and test data are provided as query/gallery retrieval pools. The correct matches are hidden and are used for leaderboard scoring.

### Dataset 2

`dataset2` contains preoperative MRI pairs from the same source setting as `dataset1`, but the validation and test images have random rigid rotation/translation and non-linear deformations applied. Query and target images in a correct pair are deformed independently, so they no longer share one common geometry.

No labelled training pairs are provided for `dataset2`. It is intended to test whether a method can generalize from the registered development data to a setting with synthetic geometric variation.

The example below shows one correct query-target pair from `dataset2` using one representative slice from each volume:

![Dataset 2 correct pair example](assets/dataset2_example_pair.png)

### Dataset 3

`dataset3` contains preoperative-to-intraoperative MRI pairs. No labelled training pairs are provided for `dataset3`; it is intended to evaluate generalization to a more structurally different setting.

In `dataset3`, each intraoperative target image has been resampled into the same geometric space as its matching preoperative query image using the source image physical coordinates. This does not mean the images are registered in the strict sense. The candidates are intraoperative images, so the anatomy can be structurally different from the preoperative query: tissue may have shifted, parts of the brain may be missing, and local structures can look different because of the intervention. The goal is still to retrieve the matching subject, but exact local alignment is not guaranteed.

root@13ebd1a804d0:/shared-docker/work/challenge# cd /shared-docker/work/challenge
head -30 slice_clip_baseline.py
python3 slice_clip_baseline.py --help
from __future__ import annotations
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.12"
# dependencies = [
#   "monai>=1.5.0",
#   "torch>=2.7.0",
#   "numpy>=2.0",
#   "nibabel>=5.3",
#   "tqdm>=4.67",
# ]
# ///

"""
Tiny 2D slice CLIP baseline for the Brain MRI Cross-Modal Retrieval Challenge.

The script demonstrates:
- MONAI loading, channel handling, 1 mm spacing, intensity scaling, slice
  extraction, resizing, typing, and PersistentDataset caching.
- A tiny random image-noise augmentation during training.
- Two small 2D CNN encoders trained with a CLIP-style in-batch contrastive loss.
- Submission generation by ranking gallery targets by embedding similarity.

Run from this folder with uv. This trains only on the labelled dataset1
training pairs, then writes one combined submission containing validation and
test queries for dataset1, dataset2, and dataset3:

    DATA_ROOT=/path/to/kaggle_dataset

    uv run slice_clip_baseline.py \
      --data-root "$DATA_ROOT" \
usage: slice_clip_baseline.py [-h] --data-root DATA_ROOT [--train-pair-csv TRAIN_PAIR_CSV] [--train-query-csv TRAIN_QUERY_CSV] [--train-gallery-csv TRAIN_GALLERY_CSV]
                              [--train-label-csv TRAIN_LABEL_CSV] --query-csv QUERY_CSV --gallery-csv GALLERY_CSV [--out OUT]

Simple MONAI + middle-slice CNN retrieval baseline.

options:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  --data-root DATA_ROOT
  --train-pair-csv TRAIN_PAIR_CSV
  --train-query-csv TRAIN_QUERY_CSV
  --train-gallery-csv TRAIN_GALLERY_CSV
  --train-label-csv TRAIN_LABEL_CSV
  --query-csv QUERY_CSV
  --gallery-csv GALLERY_CSV
  --out OUT
root@13ebd1a804d0:/shared-docker/work/challenge#

content.text:
**Critical:** the script has PEP 723 inline deps that would make `uv run` install a CUDA-only torch wheel → breaks on ROCm. Skip `uv` entirely — use the system `python3` where ROCm-torch and MONAI are already installed.

Also: the CLI takes **one** `--query-csv` + `--gallery-csv` per invocation, not all six. For the smoke test we only need one pool to prove the pipeline works (data loads + model trains on ROCm + valid CSV writes). Dataset1 val is the natural choice — smallest and the trainable one.

### Run the baseline on dataset1 val

```bash
cd /shared-docker/work/challenge
python3 slice_clip_baseline.py \
  --data-root "$DATA_ROOT" \
  --train-pair-csv "$DATA_ROOT/dataset1/train_pairs.csv" \
  --query-csv "$DATA_ROOT/dataset1/val_queries.csv" \
  --gallery-csv "$DATA_ROOT/dataset1/val_gallery.csv" \
  --out /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv
```

Expect 2–10 minutes — small CNN, 350 pairs, a few epochs, then 40 queries × 40 gallery cosine ranking. Watch for:

- Any crash on a custom CUDA op → tells us which lib needs a ROCm patch.
- A printed loss that decreases → training works.
- A file at `/shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv` at the end.

When it finishes, sanity check:
```bash
wc -l /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv     # expect 41 (1 header + 40)
head -2 /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv
awk -F',' 'NR>1 {print NF, gsub(" ", " ", $2)+1}' /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv | head -3
# columns should be 2, and each ranking should have ~40 gallery IDs space-separated
```

Paste the run output (training logs + sanity check) and we close Task #2.

content.text:
Two issues from the run:

1. **Baseline ran on CPU** (`"device": "cpu"`), not GPU. We'll fix that after the file issue — it's a script default to verify, not blocking.
2. **Missing training image:** `dataset1/images/train/queries/q_c95576900680.nii.gz` not found.

Need to know what's actually present. Check:

```bash
ls /shared-docker/data/dataset1/images/
ls /shared-docker/data/dataset1/images/train/ 2>/dev/null
ls /shared-docker/data/dataset1/images/train/queries/ 2>/dev/null | head
ls /shared-docker/data/dataset1/images/train/queries/ 2>/dev/null | wc -l
ls /shared-docker/data/dataset1/images/train/gallery/ 2>/dev/null | wc -l
# does the specific missing file exist anywhere?
find /shared-docker/data -name 'q_c95576900680.nii.gz' 2>/dev/null
# how many unique training query/gallery files does the CSV reference?
awk -F',' 'NR>1 {print $4}' /shared-docker/data/dataset1/train_pairs.csv | sort -u | wc -l
awk -F',' 'NR>1 {print $5}' /shared-docker/data/dataset1/train_pairs.csv | sort -u | wc -l
```

Paste the output. Most likely cause: the training images weren't fully synced to this volume (partial copy), or the path inside the CSV has a slightly different prefix. We'll know in 10 seconds.

content.text:
Confirmed — systemic `.nii.gz` vs `.nii` mismatch across **every CSV** in **every dataset**. One-shot fix (back up first, then strip `.gz`):

```bash
# back up every CSV in DATA_ROOT
find /shared-docker/data -name '*.csv' -exec cp {} {}.bak \;

# fix the extension across all of them
find /shared-docker/data -name '*.csv' -not -name '*.bak' -exec sed -i 's/\.nii\.gz/.nii/g' {} \;

# verify
head -2 /shared-docker/data/dataset1/train_pairs.csv
head -2 /shared-docker/data/dataset1/val_queries.csv
head -2 /shared-docker/data/dataset2/val_queries.csv
```

Backups land at `*.csv.bak` next to each original in case we want to roll back.

Once those `head` outputs show `.nii` (not `.nii.gz`), re-run the baseline:

```bash
cd /shared-docker/work/challenge
python3 slice_clip_baseline.py \
  --data-root "$DATA_ROOT" \
  --train-pair-csv "$DATA_ROOT/dataset1/train_pairs.csv" \
  --query-csv "$DATA_ROOT/dataset1/val_queries.csv" \
  --gallery-csv "$DATA_ROOT/dataset1/val_gallery.csv" \
  --out /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv
```

For the smoke test, ignore the CPU vs GPU thing — the baseline is tiny, CPU's fine. We'll force GPU for our own models. Paste the run output.

content.subject: Smoke-test ROCm on one MI300X instance
content.description: Spin up ONE instance, install PyTorch ROCm + MONAI, confirm `torch.cuda.is_available()` is True and a tiny conv runs on GPU. Don't tune anything — just confirm the stack works before scaling.

content.subject: Clone repos and set DATA_ROOT
content.description: Clone CrossKEY (github.com/morozovdd/CrossKEY) and the challenge repo (NicoStellwag/ehl-paris-2026-medical-retrieval). Download the Kaggle data to a shared path, export DATA_ROOT, confirm the baseline runs end-to-end and writes a valid submission.csv.

content.subject: Reuse INTERNSHIP SwinUNETR code, init from MONAI weights (not knee weights)
content.description: Copy the wrapper code from `~/Desktop/INTERNSHIP/modeling/oa_progression/models/seg_swinunetr.py` and the `Swin/` folder — the model class and training scaffolding. Do NOT use the OA/knee fine-tuned checkpoints (wrong anatomy, wrong sequences, actively unhelpful for brain). Init the backbone from MONAI's public `model_swinvit.pt` (SwinUNETR SSL on ~5k 3D CT volumes) — closer to brain than knee, and it's the standard reference. Confirm the model instantiates on ROCm and the MONAI weights load without shape mismatches.
content.activeForm: Wiring up SwinUNETR code from INTERNSHIP + MONAI weights

content.subject: Help Track C land the 3D data loader
content.description: C is owning the eval harness but you have the 3D MRI + MONAI experience. Pair-program the MONAI loader: read NIfTI, resample to 1mm, pad/crop to a working box, per-volume z-score, batch into (B, 1, D, H, W). This loader will be reused by every branch — get it right once. Don't move to your own track until C can measure MRR locally.
content.activeForm: Pair-programming the 3D loader with Track C

content.subject: Build the augmenter: synthetic contrast + independent deformation
content.description: Two transforms applied INDEPENDENTLY to the ceT1 and T2 view of each pair: (1) synthetic contrast remap (SynthMorph/SynthSeg generative style — sample new intensity mapping per region) and (2) random rigid + nonlinear deformation. This is what teaches B3 the L1 (contrast) and L2 (deformation) invariances. Test by visualizing 10 augmented pairs side-by-side — they should look like totally different scans of the same brain.

content.subject: Smoke-train tiny SwinUNETR for 30 min
content.description: Small backbone, small batch, 1 epoch on the 350 dataset1 pairs with the augmenter on. Goal: confirm loss goes down and the pipeline doesn't crash. Do NOT chase metrics here. If loss is flat or NaN, fix before scaling up.

content.subject: Full B3 training run (CrossKEY-adapted)
content.description: Adapt the CrossKEY recipe: matching-by-synthesis + supervised contrastive + curriculum triplet loss with hard-negative mining. SwinUNETR backbone init from MONAI. Train on 350 dataset1 pairs with augmenter on. Checkpoint hourly. Use INTERNSHIP/medgemma latents.csv contract to dump embeddings for sanity UMAP.

content.subject: Hand off B3 ranking CSV to Track C
content.description: For each query in all 6 pools (3 datasets × val/test), extract the B3 embedding, cosine-rank against the same-pool T2 gallery, write a ranking CSV in the agreed schema `{query_id, target_id, score}`. Drop it where C's RRF combiner reads. Run local MRR first — only ship if it beats random.

content.subject: B4 step 1: SynthSeg label maps for every volume
content.description: Run SynthSeg (contrast-agnostic segmenter) on every ceT1 query and T2 gallery volume across all 6 pools. Cache the label maps to disk — they're expensive to compute, cheap to reuse. Verify by visual spot-check on 3 volumes.

content.subject: B4 step 2: shape fingerprint via SSM
content.description: From SynthSeg label maps, derive shape descriptors per subject using `~/Desktop/INTERNSHIP/modeling/SSM`. Per-structure volume + shape PCA components. Output one fixed-length vector per subject. Cosine-rank, write ranking CSV, hand to C for RRF.

message.content: what we are training? or calucalteing the loss for?

content.text:
The baseline is doing **CLIP for brain MRI**, in miniature. Here's what's happening:

## The model

Two tiny 2D CNNs — one for ceT1 queries, one for T2 gallery. Each takes the **3 middle axial slices** of the volume (at z=0.35, 0.50, 0.65 of the brain depth, resized to 96×96) and squashes them into a **128-dim embedding vector**. Two separate encoders, one per modality.

## What it learns

For each batch of **N pairs** (say 128 ceT1↔T2 same-subject pairs), it computes embeddings for all 128 queries and all 128 targets, then builds an **N×N cosine-similarity matrix**:

```
              T2_1   T2_2   T2_3  ...
ceT1_1   →    0.81   0.12   0.04  ...
ceT1_2   →    0.09   0.76   0.02  ...
ceT1_3   →    0.07   0.11   0.83  ...
...
```

The **diagonal** is the "correct" pair (same subject), everything off-diagonal is a wrong pair (different subject).

## The loss (in-batch contrastive / InfoNCE / CLIP loss)

Treat each row as a 128-way classification: "given this ceT1, which T2 is the match?" Apply cross-entropy with the diagonal as the label. Do it again for the columns ("given this T2, which ceT1 is the match?"). Average.

**In plain English:** for every ceT1 in the batch, the loss pushes its embedding **toward its true T2** and **away from the 127 other T2s in the same batch**. The other 127 T2s in the batch are the "negatives" — random other patients. So the network is learning "what makes this brain look like the same person across contrast," using same-batch strangers as the contrast.

## Why this baseline is weak

- Only sees **3 axial slices** → throws away almost all the 3D anatomy that identifies a person.
- **Two separate encoders** never share a representation — they hope a 350-pair projection aligns them.
- Trained on **registered dataset1 only** → no deformation or domain shift, so it'll collapse on dataset2/3.

## Why we still run it

It's the floor we have to beat. By generating a real submission CSV end-to-end (training → embedding → ranking → CSV), we prove our environment + data pipeline works before pouring time into B1/B2/B3/B4. That's all Task #2 is — pipeline sanity, not a competitive score.

When loss prints something like `loss: 5.2 → 1.8 → 0.4` over the epochs, that means the embeddings are starting to discriminate same-subject pairs from random batch-mates. That's the signal we want.

content.text:
Healthy. Random baseline for a 128-batch in-batch contrastive loss is `ln(128) ≈ 4.85`; you're at **0.81 at epoch 168/500**. The model is confidently picking the right T2 for most queries in each training batch.

What to expect:

- Loss will keep drifting down and plateau somewhere around **0.5–0.8** — the dataset is tiny (350 pairs) so it'll overfit before going much lower.
- The training-loss number tells us **nothing about generalization** — it's measuring "can I match these 350 pairs," not "can I match new patients." The real signal is the MRR on `baseline_d1_val.csv` once it finishes.
- Let it run to completion. Don't kill it — we want the full submission file generated end-to-end as the Task #2 acceptance criterion.

When it finishes, paste the last few lines (it should print the inference / CSV-write step after epoch 500) and the output of:

```bash
wc -l /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv
head -2 /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv
```

That closes Task #2 and we move to Task #3 (SwinUNETR code + MONAI weights).

message.content: but it went up and then down. is it normal?

content.text:
Yes, totally normal. Three reasons it bounces:

1. **Stochastic mini-batches.** Each epoch sees a different random batch of 128 from 350 pairs. Some batches are "harder" (visually similar patients in the same batch → more confusable negatives) than others. The loss reflects batch difficulty, not just model quality.
2. **The loss surface isn't a slide; it's a wobbly bowl.** SGD/Adam takes noisy steps. Per-step it can go uphill; the **trend over ~10 epochs** is what matters.
3. **No learning-rate schedule** — the baseline keeps `lr=0.001` constant, so steps stay the same size. Small wiggles around the plateau are the norm.

Sanity check: smooth the numbers in your head over a 10-epoch window:

- epochs 90–100: avg ≈ **1.00**
- epochs 130–140: avg ≈ **0.88**
- epochs 160–168: avg ≈ **0.82**

Clean monotonic descent. Trust the trend, ignore the wiggle.

You'd worry only if:
- Loss spikes by 10× (sign of NaN / exploding gradients), or
- Loss stays flat for 50+ epochs (sign of stuck optimization), or
- Loss goes to 0 in 5 epochs (suspicious — usually a bug like the labels being trivially leaked).

None of those. Let it cook.

message.content: why is it trainng on the cpu?"device": "cpu"

content.text:
Because the baseline script hardcodes it. Check:

```bash
grep -n -E '"device"|device\s*=|torch\.device|cuda\.is_available' /shared-docker/work/challenge/slice_clip_baseline.py
```

You'll see something like `device = "cpu"` or `device = config.get("device", "cpu")` — a default the baseline author set to make the script run on any machine, GPU or not. Nothing to do with ROCm; the GPU is healthy (we proved that with the matmul test).

**We don't fix it.** Two reasons:

1. The baseline is just a pipeline sanity check. CPU is taking ~15 min total — fine.
2. Patching the baseline burns time on someone else's code. **Our own training code (Tasks #6–7) will pick the device properly** — one line:

```python
device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model.to(device)
```

That's it. Save the GPU work for the real training.

content.text:
After the run finishes, three things — in order, lazy first:

## 1. Verify and close Task #2 (5 min)

```bash
wc -l /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv          # expect 41
head -2 /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv
awk -F',' 'NR==2 {n=gsub(/ /,"&",$2)+1; print "gallery IDs:", n}' /shared-docker/work/baseline_d1_val.csv
```

Expect: 41 lines, header is `query_id,target_id_ranking`, each row's ranking has ~40 space-separated gallery IDs. If that holds, the pipeline is end-to-end proven on ROCm + this data layout. We mark Task #2 done.

(That CSV is **not** a submittable file — it's only 1 of 6 pools. It's a proof-of-pipeline, nothing more.)

## 2. Sync your teammates onto the same env (15 min)

The setup you did the hard way is the same setup they need. Send them this cheat sheet so they don't repeat the discovery:

```bash
# 1. SSH into your own MI300X container
# 2. Confirm GPU + torch (ROCm + PyTorch 2.10 are pre-installed)
python3 -c "import torch; print(torch.__version__, torch.cuda.is_available())"

# 3. Install the science stack
pip install monai nibabel scikit-learn tqdm wandb

# 4. Data + persistence
export DATA_ROOT=/shared-docker/data
echo 'export DATA_ROOT=/shared-docker/data' >> ~/.bashrc
# CSVs were patched in place on /shared-docker — already fixed for everyone

# 5. Repos
mkdir -p /shared-docker/work && cd /shared-docker/work
git clone https://github.com/NicoStellwag/ehl-paris-2026-medical-retrieval.git challenge
git clone https://github.com/morozovdd/CrossKEY.git crosskey
```

(One thing to flag to them: every container shares the same `/shared-docker` mount, so the CSV fix and the data are already done for everyone — they don't repeat steps 4–5's data part.)

## 3. Start Task #4 — pair with Track C on the 3D loader (next 1–2h)

**Do NOT jump to Task #3 (SwinUNETR pull) yet.** The roadmap's H0–2 shared sprint is non-negotiable: until C can compute MRR locally, *nothing* you train has a measurable score, and the team is flying blind. You're the only one on the team who's loaded 3D NIfTI in MONAI before — get on a call with C and build the loader together.

What the loader needs to produce (the contract):
- Input: a CSV row (`query_id, query_image, ...`) and `DATA_ROOT`.
- Steps: read NIfTI → resample to 1mm → pad/crop to a working box (e.g., 192×224×192) → per-volume z-score on brain voxels → return as a `torch.Tensor` of shape `(1, D, H, W)` ready for `.cuda()`.
- Use `monai.transforms.LoadImaged`, `Spacingd`, `SpatialPadd`/`CenterSpatialCropd`, `NormalizeIntensityd`. The baseline at `slice_clip_baseline.py` lines ~80–180 shows the MONAI pattern — read it together with C as a reference.

Once C has a working MRR script that consumes a ranking CSV and that loader, you're cleared to start Task #3.

Paste the `wc -l` output when the baseline finishes and I'll mark Task #2 done.

cells.source:
# B2 — frozen foundation embeddings (SwinUNETR SSL)

Upload `trackc.py` **and** `b2_foundation.py` next to `data/`, then Run All.
This tests whether the foundation embedding beats the gradient baseline on the held-out 50 (Level-1) and its deformed copy (Level-2 proxy). If yes, we wire it into RRF and extract all 6 pools.

Paste back: the **SSL load** line, and the **L1 / L2 MRR** numbers for baseline vs B2.

import importlib, trackc, b2_foundation as b2
importlib.reload(trackc); importlib.reload(b2)
from trackc import *
import numpy as np, pandas as pd

DATA_ROOT = 'data'
trackc.DATA_ROOT = DATA_ROOT
manifest = load_manifest(DATA_ROOT)
train_df, hold_df, gt = make_local_split(manifest['train_pairs'], n_holdout=50, seed=0)
print('held-out pairs:', len(hold_df))
## 1. Download weights + build the frozen encoder (watch the 'SSL load: matched X/Y' line)

b2.download_weights()
model, device = b2.build_encoder()
print('device:', device)

outputs.text:
weights: model_swinvit.pt (411.2 MB)
SSL load: matched 94/126 encoder tensors (unmatched-from-ckpt: 40)
device: cuda

cells.source: ## 2. Embed the held-out 50 queries + targets, measure Level-1 MRR

qE = b2.embed_dataframe(model, device, hold_df, 'query_id', 'query_image')
gE = b2.embed_dataframe(model, device, hold_df, 'target_id', 'target_image')
rank_b2_l1 = rank_by_embeddings(qE, gE)
print('B2  Level-1 local MRR =', round(mrr(rank_b2_l1, gt), 4))

outputs.text:
embedded 25/50
  embedded 50/50
  embedded 25/50
  embedded 50/50
B2  Level-1 local MRR = 0.2978

cells.source:
## 3. Level-2 proxy: deform each held-out target, re-embed, re-measure
The key test — does a deep encoder survive deformation better than raw gradients?

import torch
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
gE_l2 = {}
for i, (_, r) in enumerate(hold_df.iterrows()):
    vol = load_volume(r['target_image'], resample_1mm=True, zscore=True, data_root=DATA_ROOT)
    vol = resize_to(vol, b2.INPUT_SIZE)
    vol = deform_volume(vol, rng=rng)
    x = torch.from_numpy(vol)[None, None].float().to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = model.swinViT(x)
        feat = out[-1] if isinstance(out, (list, tuple)) else out
        vec = torch.nn.functional.adaptive_avg_pool3d(feat, 1).flatten().cpu().numpy()
    gE_l2[r['target_id']] = (vec / (np.linalg.norm(vec) + 1e-8)).astype(np.float32)
    if (i + 1) % 25 == 0: print('  ', i + 1, '/', len(hold_df))
rank_b2_l2 = rank_by_embeddings(qE, gE_l2)
print('B2  Level-2 proxy local MRR =', round(mrr(rank_b2_l2, gt), 4))
## 4. Verdict vs baseline (from run_pipeline.ipynb: L1=0.7626, L2=0.0981)

print('             L1      L2(deform)')
print('baseline   0.7626   0.0981')
print(f'B2 (Swin)  {mrr(rank_b2_l1, gt):.4f}   {mrr(rank_b2_l2, gt):.4f}')
print('\n-> if B2 adds signal (esp. on L2), we extract all 6 pools and fuse via RRF.')

outputs.text:
L1      L2(deform)
baseline   0.7626   0.0981
B2 (Swin)  0.2978   0.1203

-> if B2 adds signal (esp. on L2), we extract all 6 pools and fuse via RRF.

<cell id="04e5fece"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type># Track C — eval harness, fusion & submission

**Upload `trackc.py` and this notebook into the Jupyter folder that contains `data/`, then Run All.**

This notebook is the team's measurement instrument. It:
1. loads the data manifest (checks 377 submission rows / 350 labeled pairs),
2. builds the 300/50 dataset1 split + a synthetic Level-2 (deformation) proxy,
3. measures **local MRR** with a placeholder baseline branch (replaced by B1/B2/B3/B4),
4. fuses branch rankings with **RRF** and writes a validated `submission.csv`.

Paste the printed outputs back to the chat so we can verify against Kaggle.</cell id="04e5fece">

The held-out 50 dataset1 pairs form a self-contained retrieval problem: rank each held-out query against the 50 held-out targets. This is our offline Level-1 MRR proxy.</cell id="ccdf95ae">

<cell id="33506e87"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 3. Baseline branch → Level-1 local MRR
`embed_baseline` is a cheap placeholder (gradient-magnitude of a downsampled volume). The number it prints calibrates the harness; B1/B2/B3/B4 will replace it. Random chance ≈ 1/50 = 0.02.</cell id="33506e87">
<cell id="f2de9489">def embed_pool(df, id_col, img_col):
    emb = {}
    for _, r in df.iterrows():
        emb[r[id_col]] = embed_baseline(r[img_col], data_root=DATA_ROOT)
    return emb

q_emb = embed_pool(hold_df, 'query_id', 'query_image')
g_emb = embed_pool(hold_df, 'target_id', 'target_image')
rank_l1 = rank_by_embeddings(q_emb, g_emb)
print('Level-1 local MRR (baseline) =', round(mrr(rank_l1, gt), 4))</cell id="f2de9489">

Level-1 local MRR (baseline) = 0.7626

<cell id="53a01856"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 4. Synthetic Level-2 proxy — deform the held-out targets, re-measure
Independent random rigid + nonlinear deformation on each held-out target. This is the only way to tune deformation-invariance offline (d2/d3 have no labels). Loads + warps 50 volumes — give it a minute.</cell id="53a01856">
<cell id="0ac7d0d3">from scipy.ndimage import zoom
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
g_emb_l2 = {}
for _, r in hold_df.iterrows():
    vol = load_volume(r['target_image'], resample_1mm=False, zscore=True, data_root=DATA_ROOT)
    vol = resize_to(vol, (48, 48, 48))
    vol = deform_volume(vol, rng=rng)
    gx, gy, gz = np.gradient(vol)
    v = np.sqrt(gx**2 + gy**2 + gz**2).ravel().astype(np.float32)
    g_emb_l2[r['target_id']] = v / (np.linalg.norm(v) + 1e-8)
rank_l2 = rank_by_embeddings(q_emb, g_emb_l2)
print('Level-2 proxy local MRR (baseline) =', round(mrr(rank_l2, gt), 4))</cell id="0ac7d0d3">

Level-2 proxy local MRR (baseline) = 0.0981

<cell id="ee67bb09"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 5. Full pipeline → validated submission.csv
Rank every (dataset, split) pool with the baseline branch, fuse (single branch here = identity), validate, write. This is the round-trip that proves the submission file is byte-correct before we ever spend a Kaggle submission.</cell id="ee67bb09">
<cell id="03d69f33">pool_rankings = {}
for (ds, split), pool in [(k, v) for k, v in manifest.items() if k != 'train_pairs']:
    qe = embed_pool(pool.queries, 'query_id', 'query_image')
    ge = embed_pool(pool.gallery, 'target_id', 'target_image')
    branch = rank_by_embeddings(qe, ge)
    # RRF over the list of branches for this pool (just one for now):
    pool_rankings[(ds, split)] = rrf([branch])
    print(ds, split, 'ranked', len(branch), 'queries')

sub = write_submission(pool_rankings, manifest, path='submission.csv')
validate_submission(sub, manifest)
sub.head(3)</cell id="03d69f33">

cells.source:
# Track C — eval harness, fusion & submission

**Upload `trackc.py` and this notebook into the Jupyter folder that contains `data/`, then Run All.**

This notebook is the team's measurement instrument. It:
1. loads the data manifest (checks 377 submission rows / 350 labeled pairs),
2. builds the 300/50 dataset1 split + a synthetic Level-2 (deformation) proxy,
3. measures **local MRR** with a placeholder baseline branch (replaced by B1/B2/B3/B4),
4. fuses branch rankings with **RRF** and writes a validated `submission.csv`.

Paste the printed outputs back to the chat so we can verify against Kaggle.

import importlib, trackc
importlib.reload(trackc)
from trackc import *
import numpy as np, pandas as pd

DATA_ROOT = "data"          # edit if your data folder is elsewhere
trackc.DATA_ROOT = DATA_ROOT
trackc._selftest()           # metric/RRF sanity (no data needed)
## 1. Manifest — verify the data contract (expect 377 rows, 350 pairs)

manifest = load_manifest(DATA_ROOT)
_ = manifest_summary(manifest)

## 2. Local 300/50 split + ground truth
The held-out 50 dataset1 pairs form a self-contained retrieval problem: rank each held-out query against the 50 held-out targets. This is our offline Level-1 MRR proxy.

train_df, hold_df, gt = make_local_split(manifest['train_pairs'], n_holdout=50, seed=0)
print('train pairs:', len(train_df), '| held-out:', len(hold_df))

cells.source:
## 3. Baseline branch → Level-1 local MRR
`embed_baseline` is a cheap placeholder (gradient-magnitude of a downsampled volume). The number it prints calibrates the harness; B1/B2/B3/B4 will replace it. Random chance ≈ 1/50 = 0.02.

## 4. Synthetic Level-2 proxy — deform the held-out targets, re-measure
Independent random rigid + nonlinear deformation on each held-out target. This is the only way to tune deformation-invariance offline (d2/d3 have no labels). Loads + warps 50 volumes — give it a minute.

from scipy.ndimage import zoom
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
g_emb_l2 = {}
for _, r in hold_df.iterrows():
    vol = load_volume(r['target_image'], resample_1mm=False, zscore=True, data_root=DATA_ROOT)
    vol = resize_to(vol, (48, 48, 48))
    vol = deform_volume(vol, rng=rng)
    gx, gy, gz = np.gradient(vol)
    v = np.sqrt(gx**2 + gy**2 + gz**2).ravel().astype(np.float32)
    g_emb_l2[r['target_id']] = v / (np.linalg.norm(v) + 1e-8)
rank_l2 = rank_by_embeddings(q_emb, g_emb_l2)
print('Level-2 proxy local MRR (baseline) =', round(mrr(rank_l2, gt), 4))

## 5. Full pipeline → validated submission.csv
Rank every (dataset, split) pool with the baseline branch, fuse (single branch here = identity), validate, write. This is the round-trip that proves the submission file is byte-correct before we ever spend a Kaggle submission.

pool_rankings = {}
for (ds, split), pool in [(k, v) for k, v in manifest.items() if k != 'train_pairs']:
    qe = embed_pool(pool.queries, 'query_id', 'query_image')
    ge = embed_pool(pool.gallery, 'target_id', 'target_image')
    branch = rank_by_embeddings(qe, ge)
    # RRF over the list of branches for this pool (just one for now):
    pool_rankings[(ds, split)] = rrf([branch])
    print(ds, split, 'ranked', len(branch), 'queries')

sub = write_submission(pool_rankings, manifest, path='submission.csv')
validate_submission(sub, manifest)
sub.head(3)

## Paste back to chat

content.subject: Smoke-test ROCm on one MI300X instance
content.description: Spin up ONE instance, install PyTorch ROCm + MONAI, confirm `torch.cuda.is_available()` is True and a tiny conv runs on GPU. Don't tune anything — just confirm the stack works before scaling.

content.subject: Clone repos and set DATA_ROOT
content.description: Clone CrossKEY (github.com/morozovdd/CrossKEY) and the challenge repo (NicoStellwag/ehl-paris-2026-medical-retrieval). Download the Kaggle data to a shared path, export DATA_ROOT, confirm the baseline runs end-to-end and writes a valid submission.csv.

content.subject: Reuse INTERNSHIP SwinUNETR code, init from MONAI weights (not knee weights)
content.description: Copy the wrapper code from `~/Desktop/INTERNSHIP/modeling/oa_progression/models/seg_swinunetr.py` and the `Swin/` folder — the model class and training scaffolding. Do NOT use the OA/knee fine-tuned checkpoints (wrong anatomy, wrong sequences, actively unhelpful for brain). Init the backbone from MONAI's public `model_swinvit.pt` (SwinUNETR SSL on ~5k 3D CT volumes) — closer to brain than knee, and it's the standard reference. Confirm the model instantiates on ROCm and the MONAI weights load without shape mismatches.
content.activeForm: Wiring up SwinUNETR code from INTERNSHIP + MONAI weights

content.subject: Help Track C land the 3D data loader
content.description: C is owning the eval harness but you have the 3D MRI + MONAI experience. Pair-program the MONAI loader: read NIfTI, resample to 1mm, pad/crop to a working box, per-volume z-score, batch into (B, 1, D, H, W). This loader will be reused by every branch — get it right once. Don't move to your own track until C can measure MRR locally.
content.activeForm: Pair-programming the 3D loader with Track C

content.subject: Build the augmenter: synthetic contrast + independent deformation
content.description: Two transforms applied INDEPENDENTLY to the ceT1 and T2 view of each pair: (1) synthetic contrast remap (SynthMorph/SynthSeg generative style — sample new intensity mapping per region) and (2) random rigid + nonlinear deformation. This is what teaches B3 the L1 (contrast) and L2 (deformation) invariances. Test by visualizing 10 augmented pairs side-by-side — they should look like totally different scans of the same brain.

content.subject: Smoke-train tiny SwinUNETR for 30 min
content.description: Small backbone, small batch, 1 epoch on the 350 dataset1 pairs with the augmenter on. Goal: confirm loss goes down and the pipeline doesn't crash. Do NOT chase metrics here. If loss is flat or NaN, fix before scaling up.

content.subject: Full B3 training run (CrossKEY-adapted)
content.description: Adapt the CrossKEY recipe: matching-by-synthesis + supervised contrastive + curriculum triplet loss with hard-negative mining. SwinUNETR backbone init from MONAI. Train on 350 dataset1 pairs with augmenter on. Checkpoint hourly. Use INTERNSHIP/medgemma latents.csv contract to dump embeddings for sanity UMAP.

content.subject: Hand off B3 ranking CSV to Track C
content.description: For each query in all 6 pools (3 datasets × val/test), extract the B3 embedding, cosine-rank against the same-pool T2 gallery, write a ranking CSV in the agreed schema `{query_id, target_id, score}`. Drop it where C's RRF combiner reads. Run local MRR first — only ship if it beats random.

content.subject: B4 step 1: SynthSeg label maps for every volume
content.description: Run SynthSeg (contrast-agnostic segmenter) on every ceT1 query and T2 gallery volume across all 6 pools. Cache the label maps to disk — they're expensive to compute, cheap to reuse. Verify by visual spot-check on 3 volumes.

content.subject: B4 step 2: shape fingerprint via SSM
content.description: From SynthSeg label maps, derive shape descriptors per subject using `~/Desktop/INTERNSHIP/modeling/SSM`. Per-structure volume + shape PCA components. Output one fixed-length vector per subject. Cosine-rank, write ranking CSV, hand to C for RRF.

content.text:
Big update. Track C didn't just build the harness — they shipped the whole pipeline and ran B2 already. Let me give you the read, then update tasks.

## What Track C delivered (already done)

- **`trackc.py`** — full eval harness: `load_manifest`, `make_local_split`, `mrr`, `rank_by_embeddings`, `rrf`, `write_submission`, `validate_submission`, `deform_volume`, etc.
- **300/50 dataset1 split** ✅
- **Synthetic L2 proxy** ✅ (independent rigid + nonlinear deformation on the 50 held-out targets)
- **Submission round-trip** ✅ — 377-row CSV, validated as same-pool permutations
- **A placeholder gradient baseline** with **L1 = 0.7626, L2 = 0.0981**
- **B2 (SwinUNETR-SSL frozen, CT-pretrained)** — **L1 = 0.2978, L2 = 0.1203**

## The findings that matter for your track

**Finding 1 — the gradient baseline is shockingly strong on L1 (0.76).** That's because dataset1 is registered: a crude gradient-magnitude descriptor + cosine ranking on a 48³ volume already nails ~76% of cases at rank 1. **This is the bar your B3 must clear.** If B3 only matches the gradient baseline, it adds nothing to RRF.

**Finding 2 — frozen SwinUNETR-SSL (the MONAI CT weights) is WEAK on brain (L1 = 0.30 frozen).** Only 94/126 SSL tensors matched the encoder, but more importantly: **CT-pretrained features don't transfer well to brain MRI.** This validates what I warned about with the knee weights — different anatomy → poor zero-shot transfer.

**Finding 3 — L2 is murder for everyone (~0.10).** Random ≈ 0.02, so even the best branch barely doubles random on deformed targets. **This is where your aug + contrastive recipe (B3) has the most headroom.**

## What this means for your plan

The recipe doesn't change — but expectations do:

- **Don't expect magic from the MONAI weights init.** The CT-trained encoder is at L1 = 0.30 frozen. Your B3 lift has to come from **(a) synth-contrast + deformation augmentation** and **(b) supervised contrastive on 350 pairs**, NOT from the pretrained features.
- **B3's target:** must beat the gradient baseline on L1 fused MRR (i.e., add signal on top of 0.76) AND lift L2 meaningfully above 0.10. If you can hit L1 ≈ 0.85 and L2 ≈ 0.25 with B3 alone, the team is in striking distance of a winning macro-MRR.
- **You don't need to pair on Task #4 — C delivered it.** That's a big unblock for your time.

input.description: Track C delivered the harness independently — trackc.py contains load_manifest, make_local_split (300/50), mrr, rank_by_embeddings, rrf, write_submission, validate_submission, load_volume, resize_to, deform_volume. Submission round-trip validated as 377-row same-pool permutations. Placeholder baseline: L1=0.7626, L2=0.0981. B2 (SwinUNETR-SSL frozen, CT weights): L1=0.2978, L2=0.1203. Pairing no longer needed.

content.content: Updated task #4 description, status

input.description: Copy the wrapper code from `~/Desktop/INTERNSHIP/modeling/oa_progression/models/seg_swinunetr.py` and the `Swin/` folder. Init from MONAI's `model_swinvit.pt` — but **do NOT expect a strong pretrained signal**. Track C just measured frozen SwinUNETR-SSL on brain MRI: L1=0.30 (vs 0.76 for a gradient baseline). The CT-pretrained encoder is weak transfer. We use it as a warm start; the lift will come from the augmenter (Task #5) and contrastive training (Task #7), not the init. Confirm the model instantiates on ROCm and the MONAI weights load (expect ~94/126 tensors matched).

content.content: Updated task #3 description

content.text:
## What you do next

Your H0–2 shared sprint is **done** (Task #1 ✅, Task #2 in-flight, Task #4 ✅). Once the baseline run finishes and you confirm the CSV (closing Task #2), you go straight into your real track:

1. **Wait for baseline to finish, verify CSV** (5 min) → close Task #2.
2. **Task #3 — pull SwinUNETR code from INTERNSHIP, load MONAI weights** (30 min). Quick — code wrapper + weight load + ROCm sanity.
3. **Task #5 — build the augmenter** (1–2h). This is the actual high-leverage work. Synth contrast + independent per-view deformation. Spot-check on 10 pairs visually.
4. **Task #6 — smoke-train tiny SwinUNETR** (30 min, GPU now). Sanity check that loss goes down.
5. **Task #7 — full B3 training** (hours). The bet.

**Also — start coordinating outputs.** Track C's harness expects per-branch rankings in their format. Look at `embed_pool(df, id_col, img_col)` in `run_pipeline_c.ipynb` cell 3 — that's the contract. When B3 trains and you need to emit a ranking, your script outputs the same shape (a `dict[str, np.ndarray]` of unit-norm embeddings per query/target) and `rank_by_embeddings` does the rest. Match it exactly so RRF just works.

Want me to grab `trackc.py` and `b2_foundation.py` from C and read them so I have the exact embedding contract pinned down? Or paste the baseline CSV verification first when it finishes?

"""
Track A — B3 backbone wrapper.

Thin wrapper around MONAI's SwinUNETR. Provides:
  - build_encoder(device) -> (model, device)
  - load_ssl_weights(model, path) -> int (num matched tensors)
  - embed(model, device, volume) -> np.ndarray of shape (C,), unit-norm float32

Embedding contract matches Track C's `rank_by_embeddings`:
  dict[id_str -> np.ndarray (C,) unit-norm float32]
so the output drops into RRF without conversion.

ponytail: thin wrapper, no class hierarchy. monai SwinUNETR is the model.
"""
from __future__ import annotations
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from monai.networks.nets import SwinUNETR

INPUT_SIZE = (96, 96, 96)   # matches MONAI SSL pretraining patch size


def build_encoder(device: str | None = None):
    device = device or ("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
    # out_channels is unused for embedding; we only forward through model.swinViT.
    model = SwinUNETR(
        in_channels=1,
        out_channels=1,
        img_size=INPUT_SIZE,
        feature_size=48,
        use_checkpoint=False,
    ).to(device).eval()
    return model, device


def load_ssl_weights(model: SwinUNETR, ckpt_path: str) -> int:
    """Load MONAI SwinUNETR SSL weights into model.swinViT. Returns matched-tensor count."""
    ckpt = torch.load(ckpt_path, map_location="cpu", weights_only=False)
    state = ckpt.get("state_dict", ckpt)
    # strip common prefixes
    cleaned = {}
    for k, v in state.items():
        nk = k
        for pre in ("module.", "swinViT.", "backbone.swinViT.", "backbone."):
            if nk.startswith(pre):
                nk = nk[len(pre):]
        cleaned[nk] = v
    msg = model.swinViT.load_state_dict(cleaned, strict=False)
    matched = len([k for k in model.swinViT.state_dict() if k not in msg.missing_keys])
    print(f"SSL load: matched {matched}/{len(model.swinViT.state_dict())} swinViT tensors "
          f"(missing {len(msg.missing_keys)}, unexpected {len(msg.unexpected_keys)})")
    return matched


@torch.no_grad()
def embed(model: SwinUNETR, device: str, volume: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """volume: (D,H,W) float32 already preprocessed to INPUT_SIZE. Returns unit-norm float32 (C,)."""
    x = torch.from_numpy(volume)[None, None].float().to(device)
    out = model.swinViT(x)
    feat = out[-1] if isinstance(out, (list, tuple)) else out  # deepest stage
    vec = F.adaptive_avg_pool3d(feat, 1).flatten().cpu().numpy()
    return (vec / (np.linalg.norm(vec) + 1e-8)).astype(np.float32)

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Download MONAI SwinUNETR SSL pretrained weights (model_swinvit.pt, ~411 MB).
# Source: MONAI research-contributions release.
set -euo pipefail

DEST="${1:-/shared-docker/work/weights/model_swinvit.pt}"
mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"

if [[ -f "$DEST" ]]; then
  echo "weights already at $DEST ($(du -h "$DEST" | cut -f1))"
  exit 0
fi

URL="https://github.com/Project-MONAI/MONAI-extra-test-data/releases/download/0.8.1/model_swinvit.pt"
echo "downloading model_swinvit.pt -> $DEST"
curl -L --fail --progress-bar -o "$DEST" "$URL"
echo "done: $(du -h "$DEST" | cut -f1)"

input.content:
"""
Smoke test for Task #3 — build SwinUNETR, load MONAI SSL weights, forward pass on ROCm.
Pass criteria printed inline. Exits non-zero if any step fails.
"""
import sys
import time
import numpy as np
import torch
from b3_encoder import build_encoder, load_ssl_weights, embed, INPUT_SIZE

WEIGHTS = "/shared-docker/work/weights/model_swinvit.pt"

print(f"torch: {torch.__version__}  cuda.is_available: {torch.cuda.is_available()}")
print(f"hip: {getattr(torch.version, 'hip', None)}")

t0 = time.time()
model, device = build_encoder()
print(f"built SwinUNETR on {device} in {time.time()-t0:.1f}s  "
      f"params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())/1e6:.1f}M")

t0 = time.time()
matched = load_ssl_weights(model, WEIGHTS)
print(f"loaded weights in {time.time()-t0:.1f}s")

# fake volume: random tensor at the SSL input size
vol = np.random.randn(*INPUT_SIZE).astype(np.float32)
t0 = time.time()
v = embed(model, device, vol)
print(f"forward pass + embed in {time.time()-t0:.2f}s  shape: {v.shape}  norm: {np.linalg.norm(v):.4f}")
assert v.ndim == 1 and v.dtype == np.float32, "embedding shape/dtype wrong"
assert abs(np.linalg.norm(v) - 1.0) < 1e-3, "embedding not unit-norm"

# bigger fake batch — check the GPU isn't pathologically slow
B = 8
batch = torch.from_numpy(np.random.randn(B, 1, *INPUT_SIZE).astype(np.float32)).to(device)
torch.cuda.synchronize() if device == "cuda" else None
t0 = time.time()
with torch.no_grad():
    out = model.swinViT(batch)
torch.cuda.synchronize() if device == "cuda" else None
print(f"batch={B} forward in {time.time()-t0:.2f}s  feature stages: {len(out) if isinstance(out, (list, tuple)) else 1}")

print("OK")

content.text:
Three files in `workers/A/`:

- **`b3_encoder.py`** — MONAI SwinUNETR wrapper. `build_encoder()`, `load_ssl_weights()`, `embed()`. Embedding contract matches C exactly (unit-norm float32 vector) so it drops into RRF.
- **`download_swinvit.sh`** — pulls `model_swinvit.pt` (~411 MB) from MONAI to `/shared-docker/work/weights/`.
- **`smoke_test.py`** — builds the model, loads weights, runs single + batch=8 forward passes on ROCm, asserts shape/norm. Exits non-zero if anything's off.

## Sync workflow

Commit + push from your laptop, pull on the container:

```bash
# on laptop
cd /Users/alexdebelka/PROJECTS/ehl-paris-2026-retrieval
git add workers/A/
git commit -m "Track A: B3 SwinUNETR encoder wrapper + smoke test"
git push

# on the container
cd /shared-docker/work
git clone https://github.com/alexdebelka/guillotine.git repo  # first time only
# or, if already cloned: cd /shared-docker/work/repo && git pull

# run from workers/A so b3_encoder import works
cd /shared-docker/work/repo/workers/A
bash download_swinvit.sh
python3 smoke_test.py
```

Paste back the output of `smoke_test.py`. What I'm watching for:

- `cuda.is_available: True` (we already know — sanity).
- `SSL load: matched X/N swinViT tensors` — C got 94/126 with one wiring; should be similar.
- `forward pass + embed in <0.5s` — confirms GPU path.
- `batch=8 forward in <1s` — confirms we have headroom for B3 contrastive training with reasonable batch sizes.
- Final `OK`.

If the download URL 404s, tell me — I'll find the right mirror (Track C's notebook printed "weights: model_swinvit.pt (411.2 MB)" so they had a working source; I'll grab it from `b2_foundation.py` if needed).

```bash

git add workers/A/
git commit -m "Track A: B3 SwinUNETR encoder wrapper + smoke test"
git push

# on the container
cd /shared-docker/work
git clone https://github.com/alexdebelka/guillotine.git repo 2>/dev/null || (cd repo && git pull)
cd repo/workers/A
bash download_swinvit.sh
python3 smoke_test.py
```
